text: The surgeon identifies the pancreatic collection, which is visibly abutting the mesentery of the transverse colon. The walled-off pancreatic necrosis is clearly delineated intraoperatively, with the aid of intraoperative ultrasound.

Sponges are utilized for retraction and dissection, while monopolar electrocautery incises over the pancreatic collection. The surgeon suctions copious amounts of thin, purulent fluid from the collection.

After evacuating fluid from the cavity, the surgeon extends the opening in the pancreatic collection using monopolar electrocautery, carefully avoiding vessels in the transverse colon mesentery. The pancreatic debridement begins with the removal of necrotic material, which is placed on a sponge or in an endoscopic retrieval bag for later removal. The surgeon continues debridement until most of the necrotic material is removed from the collection.

The surgeon measures approximately twenty centimeters distal to the ligament of Treitz. The mesentery is incised using a bipolar vessel sealing device, and the small bowel is divided with an endoscopic stapler. The surgeon then measures twenty centimeters distal on the Roux limb to perform a side-to-side jejuno-jejunostomy. The proximal native jejunum is positioned down to the Roux limb, with an absorbable stay suture aiding in positioning and traction. Enterotomies are created with monopolar cautery, followed by the creation of a stapled side-to-side anastomosis.
The surgeon closes the common enterotomy channel using a resorbable barbed suture in a running fashion, completing the closure in a single layer.
The surgeon brings the roux limb up to the collection site and creates a long enterotomy in the small bowel, matching the length of the incision in the collection cavity.

The surgeon begins constructing the inferior wall of the cyst jejunostomy using a running absorbable barbed suture. Subsequently, the superior wall of the cyst jejunostomy is also created with the same suture technique.
The surgeon places multiple sutures to secure the anastomosis, adjusting the number based on the size of the collection and the length of the anastomosis.
